scenario: Aurora Marketplace is configuring a Camunda 8.8 order process where a developer wants to introduce a variable named order-total to track the basket value for 9,800 orders per day. The team is reviewing whether the chosen variable name will be accepted by the engine before rolling out the model to production.
question: Which response correctly classifies the variable name order-total against the Camunda 8.8 naming rules?

options.text: The name is invalid because kebab-case is not allowed; the hyphen is the FEEL subtraction operator and the team should use orderTotal or order_total instead.
The name is invalid because variable names may only contain alphanumeric characters with no hyphen, underscore, or other separator allowed between words in any case.
The name is valid because Camunda 8.8 normalises variable names by stripping non-alphanumeric characters before storage, leaving an effective stored name of ordertotal.
The name is valid because hyphens are reserved only when the variable is used in DMN tables; BPMN expressions accept kebab-case variable names without any restriction here.

a.text: Correct. The docs say variable names can be alphanumeric plus underscore and explicitly disallow kebab-case because it contains the operator -, suggesting camelCase or snake_case instead.

b.text: Incorrect. Underscores are explicitly allowed, so snake_case names such as order_total are valid; the rule only excludes hyphens and other FEEL operator characters from variable names.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented name normalisation in the engine; an invalid variable name is rejected rather than silently rewritten by stripping characters before storage.

d.text: Incorrect. The hyphen restriction applies to variable names in general because it is a FEEL operator, not just within DMN tables; BPMN expressions follow the same naming rules.

explanation: The variable-name rules allow alphanumeric characters plus underscore. kebab-case is rejected because the hyphen is the FEEL subtraction operator, so naming conventions like camelCase (orderTotal) or snake_case (order_total) are the recommended alternatives in the docs.
